steps.title: Restate the hypothesis
steps.content: Gimsa and colleagues claim the sail improved success in "underwater pursuits of prey species capable of making quick, evasive movements." Catching evasive prey underwater depends on maneuverability — the ability to change direction quickly.

steps.title: Predict the supporting result
steps.content: A finding that the sail model maneuvers better underwater than the no-sail model — for example, completing sharp turns faster — would directly back the hypothesis.

steps.title: Eliminate per choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘The model with a sail took significantly less time to complete a sharp turn while submerged than the model without a sail did.’: faster sharp turns is exactly the maneuverability advantage the hypothesis predicts. ✓
  • Choice B — ‘The model with a sail displaced significantly more water while submerged than the model without a sail did.’: about volume, not pursuit ability — irrelevant to chasing evasive prey. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘The model with a sail had significantly less battery power remaining after completing the tests than the model without a sail did.’: shows the sail costs more energy, which weakens rather than supports the hypothesis. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘The model with a sail took significantly longer to travel a specified distance while submerged than the model without a sail did.’: slower travel would undercut, not support, success in pursuits. ✗
That confirms A is correct.
